title: House Robber II
description:
This time the houses aren't in a line -- they're arranged in a circle, so the first and last houses are neighbors too. As before, adjacent houses share a security system and can't both be robbed on the same night. Given `nums[i]`, the cash sitting in house `i`, return the maximum total you can steal without ever robbing two adjacent houses (remembering that house 0 and the last house count as adjacent on the circle).

**Example**

```
nums = [2, 3, 2]     ->  3   (robbing houses 0 and 2 is illegal since they're adjacent on the circle; best is house 1)
nums = [1, 2, 3, 1]  ->  4   (rob houses 0 and 2: 1 + 3)
```
starter_code:
def rob(nums):
    # Houses are arranged in a circle; nums[0] and nums[-1] are adjacent.
    # Return the max sum obtainable without robbing two adjacent houses.
    pass
hints: Breaking the circle: robbing house 0 rules out robbing the last house, and vice versa, so the circular problem splits into two ordinary linear ones.
Run the plain (linear) House Robber DP once on nums[:-1] and once on nums[1:], and take the better of the two results.
Handle n <= 1 as a special case before splitting, since a single house has no circular constraint to worry about.

solutions.name: Brute-force subset search
solutions.explanation: Try every subset of houses (as a bitmask), reject any subset that includes two houses adjacent on the circle (including the wraparound pair house 0 / house n-1), and keep the best sum among the valid subsets.

solutions.name: Two linear House Robber passes
solutions.explanation: Since house 0 and the last house are adjacent, no valid selection can include both. So the answer is the better of: the best linear House Robber result while excluding the last house, or the best linear House Robber result while excluding the first house. Each linear pass is the standard rolling-max DP.
